retrieved_docs.title: Strangulation Injuries -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The nature of a hanging injury determines its pathophysiology. Judicial hangings from height can result in rapid deceleration forces. These mechanical forces may cause “internal decapitation,” which includes spinal cord transection, cervical vascular transection, and various spinal fractures. The classic "hangman's fracture" occurs when deceleration leads to forceful neck extension, resulting in bilateral C2 pedicle fractures. Severe forces can cause significant subluxation of C2 from C3, endangering the spinal cord. Associated hematomas may also contribute to neurological compromise. Similar hyperextension injuries have been described in motor vehicle accidents where the top of the steering wheel abruptly halts forward neck movement in the absence of an airbag.

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Autopsy -- Issues of Concern -- Aims and Objectives of a Forensic Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of death occurs either as an injury in cases of violent deaths or by disease in cases of natural deaths. The cause of death can be either natural or unnatural. For example, if a head injury is the cause of death broadly, then how the head injury occurred can be accidental, suicidal, or homicidal. Death from a head injury can result from an accidental fall from a height, or a suicidal jump from the top of a tall structure, or a deliberate push from the roof terrace, in which this last case the manner of death is considered homicidal. To be more precise, let us consider subarachnoid hemorrhage as the cause of death. Subarachnoid hemorrhage can result from a spontaneous rupture of a berry aneurysm (natural mannered death) or can be secondary to blunt force impact to the head (unnatural mannered death).

retrieved_docs.title: Blunt Head Trauma (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Falls are the most common cause of TBI, and motor vehicle-related incidents are the second leading cause of TBI. Motor vehicle-related TBI includes automobile, motorcycle, and bicycle accidents and pedestrians struck by those vehicles. Sports, recreation, and work-related injuries are the third leading cause of TBI, and assaults are the fourth leading cause of TBI. Blast injuries are the leading cause of TBI in active duty military personnel in war zones. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.content: This should be described precisely, for example, "skiing on a steep slope, lost control, and hit a tree;" or "driving a car, struck from behind, and ejected from a vehicle." In most jurisdictions, if the cause of death is not natural, the medical examiner or coroner is responsible for completing the death certificate.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Death (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Oculocephalic reflex: Turning of the eyes in the opposite direction of head movement when the head is turned from the mid position to both sides. This should not be attempted in patients with concern for cervical cord injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheobronchial Tear -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gunshot wounds generally cause injury to the cervical portion of the trachea, though any point of trachea could be injured. Stab injuries almost always involve the cervical part of the trachea. Blunt trauma to the cervical trachea could result indirectly from hyperextension injuries occurring while weightlifting or sudden deceleration during motor vehicle accidents. Direct cervical tracheal injuries result from wire strangulation while snow biking, seat belt, steering wheel, and dashboard injuries during the vehicular crash. Complex injuries involving both the above mechanisms could also happen in vehicular motor accidents as in "padded dashboard syndrome." [4] The thyroid and cricoid cartilages are most commonly involved in cervical tracheal injuries, and the area of disruption in severe injury is between the cricoid cartilage and trachea. Calcified laryngeal cartilages in adults are more likely to sustain fractures. In contrast, the flexible laryngeal cartilages are less likely injured in children when the entire brunt of trauma is targeted on the larynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Waddell Triad -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the World Health Organization (WHO) report on child injury prevention, worldwide, unintentional injuries are the leading cause of death in children between 10 to 20 years. In the United States, more than 12,000 children die each year secondary to unintentional injury. Motor vehicle injuries are the leading cause. Each year, 9.2 million children are treated in US emergency departments (EDs) for injury due to falls, but motor vehicle injuries rank among the top 15 causes of disability in children worldwide. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Sprain -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: As of 2018, whiplash injuries are estimated to affect 0.3% of people a year in the US sustained during sporting injuries, falls, and most commonly motor vehicle accidents. [8] Approximately 1 million people suffer from a whiplash-associated disorder from a motorcycle accident each year. [4] Before the introduction of seatbelts in 1990 in the UK, the rate was 42.5% of patients seen for automobile collisions with neck injuries [4] British Columbia reported that 61% of claimants to insurance companies were for neck claims in 1995. [4] The incidence is higher in women with females representing almost two-thirds of the population. [4] There is little consistency in the literature regarding epidemiology partially because of poor quality studies compounded by the complex interconnection of other factors including individual, legal, and socioeconomic factors that may influence outcomes. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Blunt Force Trauma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Proper interpretation of blunt impact injuries at autopsy is essential for accurate medicolegal testimony. Patterned blunt impact injuries may aid in determining the specific type of weapon or implement used to create the wound.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Care and Evacuation Under Fire -- Issues of Concern -- Extraction and Evacuation
retrieved_docs.content: Manual extraction is moving the patient by carrying or dragging. The patient should be moved as delicately as possible, preferably after a rapid assessment for possible injuries is completed. However, an injured SWAT officer will often need to be moved before any assessment or treatment is performed, as the risk of the rescuer being injured outweighs the risk of making the first injury worse.  When an immediate extraction is needed, only move the patient as far as is needed to get behind hard cover and out of imminent danger. There are many methods for extraction. Rescuers should do this safely and with a plan. They should protect themselves from muscle strains and know their abilities. The easiest and safest way to get a patient to hardcover may be dragging. Drag in the long axis of the body to keep the spine in alignment. Grabbing the inside of the SWAT officers vest, the rescuer will be able to support the neck with their forearms as they drag.  There are also commercially made drag systems with webbing and loops that can be applied around the ankles to help with a drag. Caution must be used when dragging an officer downstairs as gravity will cause the patient to move rapidly down the stairs. A two-rescuer approach to a stair drag is likely safer and more efficacious. A rigid or semi-rigid stretcher may be used, if further spinal protection is needed during stair extrication. Soft stretchers can also be employed and can be used one-handed on smooth surfaces (pulled/ dragged) and with two or more rescuers on concrete or other rough surfaces. Other extraction methods include the thrown rope drag and manual carries (one or two people). An armored SWAT vehicle can also be driven out to shield the officer from fire, and medical care can be provided on the scene before extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Longer deceleration pulses stretch axons, while shorter, sharper pulses more often tear bridging veins than white matter. [2] Situations that deliver these loads include high-speed vehicle collisions, falls from height, and blows that produce rapid deceleration without skull fracture. [4] DAI clusters in young adults because they experience the highest exposure to vehicular and occupational acceleration injuries. [5] Alcohol or sedative use indirectly contributes by increasing risk-taking behaviors that precipitate high-energy acceleration events.

Overall, the knowledge base connects external cause coding (firearm and motor‑vehicle categories) with specific injury types (legal‑intervention firearm injuries, cervical spine fractures, high‑energy crash injuries), their hierarchical classification under neck and head‑and‑neck injury categories, and shared morphological patterns of tissue damage that may include neurotoxic components. This integrated framework supports accurate coding, risk stratification, and coordinated care for patients presenting with these diverse but interrelated traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Head injury trauma is frequently observed in the context of a حادث سير (traffic accident). In addition, a حادث سير commonly co‑occurs with وجع في الكتف (shoulder pain). Consequently, head injury trauma, traffic accidents, and shoulder pain are interrelated clinical findings that often appear together in patients involved in vehicular collisions, underscoring the importance of evaluating both cranial and musculoskeletal injuries after such events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Injury due to legal intervention by firearm and injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of aircraft are both linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is itself associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that such traumatic events may involve neurotoxic processes alongside structural tissue injury. Recognizing the connection between these mechanisms can inform clinical assessment and management of patients with firearm‑related legal interventions or aircraft‑related trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1652
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion and the injury whose intent is undetermined but resulted from an aircraft crash are both described as having an associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that the tissue damage observed in these severe injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. This interconnected understanding highlights that both the cervical spine fracture with cord involvement and the ambiguous aircraft‑related injury share a common pathological feature of tissue damage, which can contribute to neurotoxic effects that are relevant for diagnosis, management, and prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 166

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft” is linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). In turn, the tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). This relationship indicates that injuries resulting from an aircraft crash, even when the intent is unclear, may involve underlying tissue damage that can give rise to neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of evaluating both structural and neurotoxic consequences in the clinical assessment and management of such trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2591
retrieved_docs.content: The condition إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is clinically associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption); alcohol use increases the likelihood of sustaining a closed head injury, and patients presenting with such injuries often have a history of recent alcohol intake. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians when assessing trauma patients, guiding both acute management and preventive counseling regarding alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: External cause coding links specific mechanisms of injury to their clinical manifestations. “External cause codes: Firearm” classifies “Injury due to legal intervention by firearm,” which is associated with the morphology of “تَلَف الأنسجة” (tissue damage). Similarly, injuries of uncertain intent caused by the crashing of an aircraft or a motor vehicle are also linked to “تَلَف الأنسجة,” indicating that severe mechanical forces commonly result in tissue damage that may progress to “سمية عصبية” (neurotoxicity). Fractures of the cervical spine with cord lesions are identified as “كسر في الفقرة الرقبية” (cervical vertebra fracture) and are categorized under “إصابة في الرقبة” (neck injury). This fracture type is further classified among “other fractures” and frequently co‑occurs with “كسر الحوض” (pelvic fracture), highlighting the need for comprehensive assessment of axial skeletal injuries. “Head and neck injury” is also placed under the broader parent category of “إصابة في الرقبة,”
